western.definition: Mechanical/electronic tools and systems; usually requires external energy inputs; often replaces human/biological processes.

relational.definition: Any systematic method for accomplishing goals; includes biological, social, technological, and knowledge systems; often enhances rather than replaces natural processes.
assumptions: Technology as tool, not technology as replacement.

functional_comparison.relational: Systematic methods and tools built with knowledge of tool building
Acoustic triangulation, star navigation, plus technology if available

hidden_variables.name: Maintenance requirements
hidden_variables.description: Ongoing inputs the technology needs to keep functioning.
hidden_variables.why_western_misses_it: Capital cost is foregrounded; lifecycle cost is treated as an externality.

hidden_variables.name: Breakdown resilience
hidden_variables.description: How the wider system performs when the technology fails.

hidden_variables.name: Knowledge transmission
hidden_variables.description: Whether the practice survives without the artifact.

hidden_variables.name: Relationship to natural systems
hidden_variables.description: Whether the technology cooperates with or displaces existing biological/ecological processes.

translation_protocol.when_to_translate: When 'technology' is being defined narrowly as manufactured tools and biological/social methods are being implicitly excluded.
translation_protocol.how: Ask: 'What systematic method are we comparing against? Including biological and social methods, what is the actual choice space?'
